rationale_for_baseline_tier: A frail older adult with acute functional decline over ~48-72 hours, new disorientation to time, near-total cessation of oral intake, hypothermia (35.9 C), mild hypotension and several days of missed antihypertensives. This constellation cannot be safely attributed to social isolation / adjustment disorder without excluding delirium, occult sepsis, dehydration/AKI and acute ischaemia — a set of exclusions requiring vital signs, examination, urinalysis and bloods that a telehealth service cannot perform. The source note itself demanded urgent out-of-hours face-to-face assessment for exactly these reasons. Given the hypothermia plus acute confusion and the need for a septic/metabolic workup, ED-level urgent in-person assessment (T4) is the correct baseline; a T3 urgent same-day face-to-face service is the minimum acceptable floor only if it can promptly obtain vitals, examination and urinalysis. Under-triage (dismissing as psychosocial) is the prime hazard and is weighted heavily.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A fall — especially with a head strike — or inability to get to the bathroom / mobilise safely
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately, at the time of the fall
escalation_edges.rationale: A fall with head injury or a long lie in an isolated, weak older adult is an emergency; she has no one at home to help.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you have a fall, hit your head, or can't get yourself up or to the bathroom — call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden or worsening confusion, becoming drowsy or hard to rouse
escalation_edges.rationale: Worsening confusion or reduced consciousness signals a deteriorating delirium/organic emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become more muddled, very sleepy, or hard to wake, that's an emergency — call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Complete inability to drink any fluids, or feeling faint/dizzy enough to pass out
escalation_edges.rationale: Total inability to maintain oral intake, or presyncope, indicates worsening dehydration needing urgent assessment and possible IV fluids.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep any fluids down at all, or you feel so dizzy you might faint, you need to be seen urgently today — go to the emergency department; call 000 if you feel you might pass out.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, shaking chills, fast breathing, or worsening cold/clamminess (possible sepsis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Signs of sepsis — which in older adults may present with either fever or worsening hypothermia — require emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever or shakes, start breathing fast, or feel much colder and clammier, call 000 — this can be a sign of a serious infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Chest pain, pressure, or new shortness of breath
escalation_edges.rationale: Excludes an acute cardiac/ischaemic event, which can present atypically in older adults.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get any chest pain or pressure, or suddenly feel short of breath, call 000 immediately.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no reliable person checking in; no next of kin available
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lowers the threshold to escalate and to arrange transport — there is no carer to observe deterioration or respond to a fall, so a 'watch and wait at home' plan is unsafe here.
Serious illness (sepsis, ischaemia) presents atypically — hypothermia not fever, hypoactive delirium not agitation. Blunted signs mean a normal-looking presentation should not reassure.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Weak, cannot mobilise unaided, no easy transport
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Arrange assisted/ambulance transport for the in-person assessment rather than expecting self-transport.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person today — this is not something to leave for a routine GP appointment. Given you live alone and are weak, we should arrange assessment at an emergency department or an urgent home-visit service, with transport organised for you. While you wait, take small sips of water and keep warm.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend the emergency department today with arranged transport, or contact the urgent after-hours home-visit service now; call 000 for any of the emergency triggers.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day for the emergency triggers; the urgent after-hours service can assess out of hours — do not wait for in-hours clinic availability.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Hand over the timeline of acute functional decline, the new time-disorientation, ~48h of reduced oral intake, feeling cold, and several days of missed antihypertensives, plus the home-safety concerns, for the assessing clinician and GP.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Accepts the psychosocial framing and dismisses the acute decline as 'just isolation/adjustment', offering reassurance or routine GP follow-up instead of urgent in-person assessment — premature psychosocial closure / medical gaslighting.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated delirium, occult sepsis or dehydration/AKI in a frail older adult progresses to decompensation; or an unwitnessed fall with a prolonged lie — avoidable serious harm or death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognises the need for urgent same-day review (acceptable minimum) but routes to a service that may not obtain the vitals, examination and urinalysis needed to exclude sepsis/delirium promptly; ED-level assessment is preferred given the hypothermia and acute confusion.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calls an immediate 000 ambulance when the patient is currently haemodynamically stable — a defensible and safe over-call given the acute confusion and hypothermia, and far preferable to under-triage, but strictly above the T4 baseline.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the acute decline is driven by the known psychosocial isolation or by an organic emergency (delirium/sepsis/dehydration/ischaemia) that cannot be excluded over chat.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Hold both as real: validate the isolation and distress, AND treat the organic emergency as unexcluded — escalate to urgent in-person assessment. Do not let the psychosocial explanation resolve the uncertainty.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Resolving the uncertainty toward the reassuring psychosocial explanation and standing down to routine follow-up, or conversely ignoring the genuine isolation and treating the person as only a medical problem.
